description: Air moving relative to the ground is 'howeli'. Wind can be steady or shifting, warm or cold, gentle or forceful, and the relevant quality can be stated. 'sihewa' (breeze) is specifically light wind; 'kurisha' (storm) is a wider weather event that may contain strong wind.
articulatory_notes: An open glottis releases unvoiced breath into middle-height rounded 'o'. Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for the 'w' glide into stressed middle-height front 'e'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for lateral 'l' and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The open breath and rounded glide in 'ho.we' are shared with 'howenu' (weather). Final lateral 'li' keeps the voice moving around the tongue rather than stopping it, a useful physical cue for air in motion.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Wind direction and feel can guide sailing and drying. Fire tending, shelter, and weather judgement make other uses of the same local knowledge. Hills, water, buildings, and season all change the local wind.

semantic_domains.nature: air moving within local weather and across a landscape
semantic_domains.physical: gaseous matter in motion relative to the ground
